they were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judaea and Samaria - The Savior had prophesied of Jerusalem's destruction - the Christians got out of town. It is interesting that the Savior's prophecy not only saved them from the Roman destruction but also the Jewish persecution. (LLD)

In the case of Philip, when he went down to Samaria [he] was under the spirit of Elias. He baptized both men and women. When Peter and John heard of it, they went down and laid hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost. This shows the distinction between the two powers. (Joseph Smith, 1839 History; PJS, 1:372.)

In the case of Philip, when he went down to Samaria [he] was under the spirit of Elias. He baptized both men and women. It is this that the Prophet Habakkuk refers to when he speaks of the Lord coming in glory, and says: “He had horns coming out of his hand: and there was the hiding of his power.” Horns we know in the Scriptures are often used as figures to represent power. Horns in the Apocalypse and in the prophecies of Daniel represent kingdoms and dynasties, and…

impostor cannot really confer the Holy Ghost. That comes from God. No man can bestow the gift of the Holy Ghost upon anyone; that is the gift of God. We read about a man who thought he could purchase this power. He offered the apostles money for it. But Peter said unto him, “Thy money perish with thee because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money.” Now, here we have…

"Philip was found at Azotus." (Acts 8:40.) The name of Old Testament Ashdod, one of the five major cities of the Philistines, was changed in Hellenistic times to Azotus. It was the scene of some of Philip's missionary activity among hellenized Jews, Greeks, and Romans residing there. "The angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, Arise, and go toward the south unto the way that goeth down from…

impostor cannot really confer the Holy Ghost. That comes from God. No man can bestow the gift of the Holy Ghost upon anyone; that is the gift of God. We read about a man who thought he could purchase this power. He offered the apostles money for it. "Philip was found at Azotus." (Acts 8:40.) The name of Old Testament Ashdod, one of the five major cities of the Philistines, was changed in Hellenistic times to Azotus. It was the scene of some of Philip's missionary activity among hellenized Jews, Greeks, and Romans residing there.
